TWEWY, Banner Saga, FFVII, and FFXV all point to strong sensitivity to style and soundtrack identity.
ICO's accompanied journey, FFXV's road-group dynamic, and Banner Saga's caravan framing all point to valuing relational travel stories.
ICO's ending and other liked games suggest the emotional landing matters a lot, even when the path has friction.
ICO required heavy guide use and NieR: Automata was paused early because the opening was hard to understand.
ICO was liked a lot despite unoptimized gameplay because its concept and overall feel were distinct.
Daxter and Portal 2 both landed partly because humor helped the experience stay engaging.
Ori, Daxter, and parts of Kingdom Hearts point to strong enjoyment of agile movement and responsiveness.
Ori stood out specifically for the feeling of achievement, dynamism, and progression through action.
Uncharted's open-area side content did not attract the user, and FFXV hunts/endgame were also unappealing.
FFXII license board and gambits, Crisis Core materias, Mario + Rabbids 1 progression, and TWEWY pin/equipment systems all landed strongly.
Detroit Become Human and Heavy Rain both landed strongly because of decisions and ramifications.
Ivalice, the judges, and broader Final Fantasy lore preferences show strong attachment to rich settings and political texture.
Mass Effect trilogy, FFXV, Banner Saga, and other favorites point to strong attachment to companion dynamics across a long-form journey.
Ni no Kuni was highlighted partly for its plot twist or story shift, which matches earlier responses to emotionally charged narrative turns.
Tekken Dark Resurrection, Dissidia, and other long-played PSP titles suggest content variety and a fun core loop can sustain engagement even outside story-first games.
John Wick Hex and No More Heroes seemed like theoretical fits but still failed, suggesting style alone is not enough without stronger emotional or narrative attachment.
Marvel Ultimate Alliance 3, John Wick Hex, and No More Heroes all point to low tolerance for loops that feel repetitive or padded without strong payoff.
NEO: The World Ends with You was completed despite some repetition because the story carried it, similar to other narrative-forward cases.
Persona 5 Royal slowed down in backlog at the start, reinforcing earlier friction with slow starts like Kingdom Hearts II and other slower openings.
13 Sentinels, Fire Emblem: Three Houses, TRIANGLE STRATEGY, Banner Saga, and Mario + Rabbids Kingdom Battle all landed strongly.
Expedition 33 and Bravely Default both landed in atmosphere and systems, but the user bailed when difficulty stopped the story from flowing.
Advance Wars kept attracting the user conceptually, but boredom set in; in contrast, tactics with stronger story or cast investment land much better.
Baten Kaitos, SMT V, and Astral Chain all attracted through world, lore, or style, but still failed to sustain play momentum.
Donkey Kong Country 2 was fully completed with over 100 percent, suggesting challenge is welcome when the feel is precise and rewarding.
The user often finishes story interest through YouTube after dropping the game itself when difficulty, repetition, or pacing take over.
Ori plus Castlevania Aria of Sorrow and Dawn of Sorrow strongly confirm love for map unlocking, backtracking payoff, and progression via new abilities.
Link's Awakening and Twelve Minutes both became guide-heavy forced completions instead of enjoyable discovery.
Resident Evil Village landed strongly due to atmosphere, pacing, and gameplay.
Doki Doki Literature Club Plus and Catherine both show attraction to unusual psychological or meta-driven premises.
Breath of the Wild stalled mid-run even with hacks, and other open or drift-heavy games often lose momentum unless the narrative or progression pull is strong.
Resident Evil 4 and Resident Evil Village both landed strongly through pacing, atmosphere, and satisfying combat progression.
Bayonetta 2 and Bayonetta 3 did not sustain enjoyment, reinforcing that pure stylish action is not enough by itself.
Pikmin 1 through 4 all landed strongly and remain replay candidates, suggesting strong affinity for structured objective play with planning and execution.
Metroid Fusion, Castlevania Aria of Sorrow, Dawn of Sorrow, Circle of the Moon, Harmony of Dissonance, and Hollow Knight all point to a very stable love for exploration-plus-mastery loops.
The user repeatedly marks major JRPGs, tactics RPGs, and PS1 story-heavy classics as games they still want to play, even when not started yet.
Metroid Fusion, multiple Castlevanias, and Hollow Knight show that the user strongly enjoys map completion and 100 percent style goals in exploration-heavy action games.
Chrono Trigger, Ghost Trick, 999, Parasite Eve, Xenogears, Vagrant Story, and Disco Elysium indicate strong future interest in story-heavy classics and mysteries.

Resident Evil 4, Resident Evil 7, Dead Space, Dead Space 2, Silent Hill, and Metal Gear Solid all suggest high engagement with tightly paced atmospheric action-horror and stealth-horror adjacent games.
Blasphemous, Hollow Knight, Metroid Dread, Metroid Fusion, and multiple Castlevanias suggest strong tolerance for challenge when exploration and combat flow stay satisfying.
Spider-Man, Jedi Survivor, The Last of Us 1 and 2, A Plague Tale games, Guardians of the Galaxy, and Max Payne 3 all reinforce strong fit for focused story-driven campaigns.
Pentiment, The Quarry, Doki Doki Literature Club Plus, Heavy Rain, and Detroit Become Human all landed strongly despite lower mechanical intensity.
Ridge Racer 1 and 2 plus Need for Speed Carbon, ProStreet, and The Run all landed well and were fully completed.
Devil May Cry 4, Metal Gear Rising, and Zone of the Enders suggest stylish action can land when the campaign and fantasy are compelling, despite Bayonetta 2 and 3 not landing.
Prince of Persia entries, Gears of War, Final Fantasy XIII, Max Payne 3, and The Last of Us all show strong fit for focused campaigns when pacing stays high.
Pentiment and L.A. Noire both landed well, suggesting strong fit for mystery and case-based stories even when combat is minimal.
